A1.emotional_beat: A slow unease settles in when the memo calls Mara Ellison’s death a likely ‘accidental fall’ yet confidently notes ‘no active oxygen alarm pages’ in Stairwell 4B—an oddly tidy detail for a badge-locked cGMP facility.

detective_annotations.style_hint: digital yellow highlight with a thin black outline

detective_annotations.content: Facilities says ‘no O₂ alarm pages’… does that mean nothing happened, or that paging was OFF? Ask Briggs (POI-04).
detective_annotations.style_hint: typed callout box, small sans-serif, slightly angled

detective_annotations.content: (Maybe just a missed page / system glitch?) Don’t assume intent yet — but a ‘quiet’ stairwell death in Building 7 feels wrong.

A1.reading_order_note: Read first. This is the ‘official accident’ baseline that later documents (especially C1/C2) will undercut.

A2.emotional_beat: The case snaps into focus as a suspect map: four living POIs with overlapping pressure and permissions, and the realization that ‘QA sign-off’ (Raina Patel) is as dangerous a lever as security access or money.

detective_annotations.content: QA SIGN-OFF PATH → Patel (POI-05). Deviation ownership + release authority = leverage.
detective_annotations.style_hint: thin red digital circle with a small arrow

detective_annotations.content: Kline (POI-02): money + deal pressure. Reyes (POI-03): access + fear. Briggs (POI-04): alarms/logs. Patel (POI-05): audit trail. Where do pressure + permissions overlap?
detective_annotations.style_hint: clean gray callout box with bullet-like line breaks

A2.reading_order_note: Best read immediately after A1 so names (Ellison, Kline, Reyes, Briggs, Patel) stick before you hit the noisier public/OSINT material in A3.

A3.emotional_beat: The ‘miracle cure’ hype turns sour when a semi-insider voice drops specific language—‘QA deviation’ and ‘cleaning PDFs since January’—making it feel like the real crime might be paperwork first, violence second.

detective_annotations.content: @bench2board: ‘QA logged a deviation’ + ‘cleaning PDFs since January’ — too specific for random trolling.
detective_annotations.style_hint: digital neon highlight (cyan) on dark-mode UI

detective_annotations.content: If this is real, what’s the deviation ID? DEV-??? Request internal email exports / eQMS history.
detective_annotations.style_hint: small red question mark icon + short callout

A3.reading_order_note: Read before A5 so the Ledger’s polished language lands as a contrast to the rumor of ‘cleaned’ documentation.

A4.emotional_beat: Handling the floor plan makes the death feel engineered: Stairwell 4B isn’t just a place—it’s a controllable pocket with a keyed Utility Closet U-4B, an O₂ monitor, and a camera blind spot big enough to hide a decision.

detective_annotations.content: Blind spot at landing (CAM-4B-LND) — whoever picked 4B knew this gap.
O₂ Monitor ↔ Utility Closet U-4B (KEYED) — access list? key control logs?

detective_annotations.content: ‘SP-4B’ / ‘Paging relay panel’ mentioned — match exact device names if/when we pull system history.

A4.reading_order_note: Read after A1 (so Stairwell 4B matters) and before any deep log documents later; this is the spatial key that makes timestamps feel physically plausible.

A5.emotional_beat: The business press optimism becomes eerie because it’s timestamped (2026-03-20) and full of careful compliance phrasing—especially Kline’s line about ‘any deviation is assessed and documented’—words that feel like a pre-written defense once you remember Ellison is dead by morning.

detective_annotations.content: Printed 2026-03-20 09:10 — THIS is the narrative Helixure needed to protect.
Kline quote: ‘any deviation is assessed and documented’ — flag this. If docs were ‘cleaned,’ the lie is in what’s missing, not what’s said.
